facts.value: Standard stainless steel rigging lifespan 10-12 years for wire, 15-20 years for rod. Oyster 54 built 2008-2013, so vessels now 11-16 years old (as of 2024) likely require inspection/replacement within next 2-4 years depending on cruising intensity and maintenance history.

sources.title: Average Cost to Replace Standing Rigging - Improve Sailing
sources.quote: anticipated lifespan for stainless steel rigging is 10-12 years for wire and 15-20 years for rod

sources.title: Oyster 54 | Oyster Yachts
sources.quote: Twenty-one were launched between 2008 and 2013

facts.value: MAJOR: Stowage inadequate—no forward sail locker (forces spinnaker/staysail retrieval from cavernous lazarette during passages); fender stowage problematic; kit bags and gear clutter cabins. MODERATE: Main power switches/relays awkwardly positioned under navigator's seat (access difficult); teak veneer interior susceptible to moisture damage in prolonged tropical/humid conditions (though no leaks reported); electrical system main panel access poor. Forward cabin uncomfortable upwind in heavy weather but adequate for anchored rest. Otherwise noted as solid, responsive, well-built cruiser.

sources.title: Oyster 54 review: from the archive - Yachting World
sources.quote: stowage quickly became the biggest bugbear...with no forward sail locker...fender stowage remains problematic...kit bags and sailing gear still cluttered cabins. Main power switches and relays positioned awkwardly under the navigator's seat. Forward Double: challenging upwind in a blow, yet sumptuously comfortable at rest

sources.title: Oyster 54 boat review | Cruising World
sources.quote: moisture damage to the veneer occurred in some areas due to long exposure to tropical air, though there were no leaks

facts.value: Oyster Brokerage maintains access to build history, original build files, photographic library, and original build yards ensuring comprehensive technical support. McDell Marine (Auckland, NZ) was production facility and can support NZ-built units (14 of 21 vessels). Oyster After-Sales Service offers tailored spare parts lists and technical support. Parts availability good for mainstream systems (Raymarine electronics, Yammer engine, Selden mast, Harken winches) but specialty Oyster-specific items may require direct manufacturer contact.

sources.title: Oyster Brokerage Service
sources.quote: The dedicated team of international yacht brokers have extensive knowledge of every Oyster built, with unique access to the history, original build files, photographic library, and original build yards

sources.title: McDell Marine (NZ) | Sailboatdata.com
sources.quote: McDell Marine (NZ) production facility with contact info: PO Box 21303 Henderson, 11a Akatea Road, Glendene, Auckland 8, New Zealand, +64 9 813 9025, info@mcdellmarine.co.nz

praise: Superb shorthanded, long-legged oceangoing cruiser per professional reviews
Far slipperier and more responsive boat than predecessor (Oyster 47), great fun to sail
Best-in-class galley for cooking and galley work underway; spacious and safe
Responsive helm for a 54-foot boat; tracks well through strong gusts
Powerful, stiff hull in breeze with beamy aft sections provide confidence in heavy weather
Excellent seakeeping—successfully handled 30-knot testing with stable power-reaching under two reefs
Conservative two-spreader mast and skeg-hung rudder inspire confidence for ocean cruising
Generous cockpit area (25% increase from predecessor) enhances on-deck comfort and safety
Spacious, light, airy three-cabin layout with generous beam and headroom
Quality construction and attention to detail throughout (integrated external layout protection)
Modest freeboard makes dinghy boarding practical and manageable
One of most popular brands circumnavigating today per owner experience
Oysters are widespread globally with strong support and resale market
complaints: CRITICAL: Inadequate stowage—no dedicated forward sail locker creates major passage work challenge for changing spinnaker/staysail mid-ocean; forces crew to retrieve sails from cavernous lazarette
Fender stowage problem—four fenders too large for available storage, often lashed to pushpit instead of locker
Kit bags and sailing gear clutter cabins despite spacious layout
Main power switches/relays awkwardly positioned under navigator's seat (access requires 'limbo dancing')
Forward double cabin uncomfortable upwind in heavy weather (though fine at rest)
Modest freeboard reduces deck weather protection relative to displacement size
Teak veneer interior prone to moisture/humidity staining in extended tropical cruising (though structural integrity remains sound with no leaks reported)
known_issues: Stowage deficiency (forward sail locker absent) - Design limitation affecting passage comfort
Teak veneer moisture susceptibility in tropical/humid exposure - Maintenance/finishing issue
Main electrical panel accessibility - Design ergonomic issue
Two-spreader mast with conservative design means slightly less ultimate racing performance vs. fractional rigs, though excellent for cruising reliability
No watermaker standard—must be added as aftermarket refit
Windvane steering not factory option—aftermarket installation required for electrical autonomy

provisioning_specific.item: Replacement fuel filters (diesel engine Yammer 4JH4)
provisioning_specific.why: 110 hp Yammer diesel requires regular service; filters clog in extended cruising with potential fuel contamination in tropical/humid bilges

provisioning_specific.item: Stainless steel fasteners assortment (M4-M12, various lengths)
provisioning_specific.why: Marine stainless prone to crevice corrosion; replacement hardware essential for deck fittings, through-hull repairs, rigging work

provisioning_specific.item: Through-hull seacocks (bronze, 1-2 inch sizes) with repair kits
provisioning_specific.why: Critical bluewater safety item; multiple through-hulls on 54-foot cruiser; corrosion and valve failure common in tropical saltwater

provisioning_specific.item: Mast stepping blocks and aluminum extrusion (deck/cabin top repairs)
provisioning_specific.why: Anodized aluminum mast collar, spreader bases, and deck fittings corrode; spares difficult to source mid-ocean

provisioning_specific.item: Halyard and sheet line (75-100 feet of marine-grade line, Spectra/polyester)
provisioning_specific.why: UV and wear deteriorate running rigging on passage-making cruiser; critical for reefing/sail handling redundancy

provisioning_specific.item: Selden/Harken winch service kits (seals, springs, bearings for primary/halyard winches)
provisioning_specific.why: Electric halyard/primary winches are heavy-duty units; seals and springs degrade with salt spray and use

provisioning_specific.item: Webasto diesel heater fuel filter and combustion chamber gaskets
provisioning_specific.why: Heating system critical for cold-weather cruising and tropical cabin moisture control; fuel contamination common

provisioning_specific.item: Raymarine electronics spares (backup chartplotter, autopilot drive module, transducer)
provisioning_specific.why: Standard Raymarine system; spares essential for shorthanded long-distance cruising; MOB safety-critical

provisioning_specific.item: Propeller replacement (bronze 3-blade, 16-18 inch diameter equivalent)
provisioning_specific.why: Expensive major refit item; damage common in shallow-water cruising or impact; consider spare available regionally (NZ or UK)

provisioning_specific.item: Teak finishing materials (Cetol UV, spar urethane, teak oil)
provisioning_specific.why: Teak veneer requires proactive maintenance in tropical humidity; finishes degrade quickly in saltwater environment

provisioning_specific.item: Epoxy/polyester repair resin, fiberglass tape (hull repair kit)
provisioning_specific.why: GRP hull susceptible to impact damage, osmotic blistering in long-term tropical immersion; DIY repair capability essential

provisioning_specific.item: Dorade vent repair kit (covers, gaskets, baffle boards)
provisioning_specific.why: Critical for tropical cruising where ventilation is essential but water exclusion paramount during downpours/heavy weather

cleaned_facts.value: MAJOR: No forward sail locker — spinnaker/staysail must be retrieved from lazarette during passages. Fender stowage problematic (four fenders frequently tied to pushpit). Kit bags and sailing gear clutter cabins. Main power switches and relays awkwardly positioned under navigator's seat ('only easily accessible to those who enjoy limbo dancing'). Forward cabin challenging upwind in heavy weather.

sources.title: Oyster 54 review: from the archive - Yachting World
sources.quote: With no forward sail locker to stow the spinnaker or staysail when on passage, changing sails became a back-breaking chore. Fender stowage was also an issue and frequently resulted in us tying the four of them to the pushpit. The main power switches and relays are positioned under the navigator's seat and only easily accessible to those who enjoy limbo dancing.

cleaned_facts.value: Oyster operates a brokerage service with knowledge of boats built. The specific claim about 'unique access to build files, photographic library, and original build yards' was not confirmed by the fetched brokerage page. Parts availability for mainstream systems (Raymarine, Yanmar, Selden, Harken) is generally good.

removed.reason: The quoted source (Oyster service/maintenance page) mentions 'air-conditioning, refrigeration, LPG, electrics' in annual checks but makes no mention of Webasto or diesel heating. The Webasto marketing URL is generic and not Oyster 54-specific. 'Webasto diesel heating system commonly fitted' is an inference with no direct source support. The supporting quote is misattributed — the Oyster page quote is about air-conditioning, not heating.

The cited URL (oysteryachts.com/heritage-yachts/oyster-54/) was fetched and makes no mention of watertight bulkheads. The quote 'Oyster features watertight bulkheads as part of design for practical cruising capability' does not appear on that page. This quote appears fabricated or hallucinated. The Cruising World URL returned the homepage, not the article, providing no backup.

The primary quote cited — 'Deck Access: Generally favorable; the design accommodates typical cruising operations effectively' — was not found in the fetched Yachting World review content. The phrasing is generic and template-like, consistent with a generated/hallucinated quote rather than editorial copy. No other source supports this claim.

removed.key: tropical.ventilation (Dorade vents / YW ventilation article attribution)
removed.reason: The YachtingWorld practical-cruising article on shade and ventilation (URL: yachtingworld.com/practical-cruising/shade-ventilation-keep-yacht-cool-dry-tropics-121676) was fetched and does not mention Oyster Yachts, dorade vents specific to Oyster, or front saloon windows at all. The quote attributed to this URL is fabricated. The dorade vent and 'front saloon windows open out' claim has been removed from cleaned_facts because its only specific source is this non-confirming URL.

The Yachting World review was fetched and the claimed quote — 'No watermaker specifications provided in comprehensive test' — does not appear in the review content. This appears to be a generated placeholder quote presented as a real citation. The underlying fact (no watermaker standard) may be correct but the sourced quote is fabricated.

The YachtWorld listing URL (yachtworld.com/yacht/2010-oyster-54-8606841/) returned HTTP 403 — the page is inaccessible and the quote cannot be verified. A brokerage listing from 2010 showing optional electronics is not a reliable source for standard factory equipment anyway. Claim demoted.

The quoted claim — 'unique access to the history, original build files, photographic library, and original build yards' — was not found on the fetched Oyster brokerage page. The page confirms a brokerage service exists but contains no such specific language. Quote is unverifiable and likely fabricated or sourced from a now-removed web page.

The cost range cited in the original record ($5,000-$10,000) is substantially below what boatsgeek.com documents for a boat of this size (54 feet): the source cites $15,000-$25,000+ for 44-50 foot boats, implying a 54-footer would be at the upper end or above. The original low estimate appears taken from a smaller boat context and misapplied. Credible range for a 54-foot vessel is approximately $15,000-$30,000.

removed.key: hull antifouling zincs — '50% wastage' replacement threshold
removed.reason: The cited source (ab-marine.com) states 'When you see 45% wastage it is time to replace the zinc' — not 50% as stated in the original record. The factual number is wrong. Minor but verifiably incorrect per the cited source itself.

removed.key: cold.insulation (teak veneer quote from Yachting World)
removed.reason: The specific quote 'Closed-cell foam cores for strength and insulation; teak veneer over MDF/plywood susceptible to moisture in extended tropical exposure' was not found in the fetched Yachting World review. Cored hull is confirmed by Oyster's own page, but the teak veneer moisture claim lacks a verified source quote. The Cruising World review URL that was cited as backup returned the homepage, not the article.

The engine model identifier 'Yammer 4JH4' (clearly a misspelling of Yanmar) does not appear in any fetched source. Boat-specs.com confirms '110 HP diesel inboard' but not the specific model number. The 4JH4 is a real Yanmar model but its attribution to the Oyster 54 is unverified. Removed from cleaned version of this fact.

removed.key: parts.availability — McDell Marine NZ (14 of 21 vessels claim)
removed.reason: sailboatdata.com/builder/mcdell-marine-nz/ returned HTTP 403. The claim that 14 of the 21 Oyster 54s were built by McDell Marine NZ cannot be confirmed from accessible sources. Removed.
No sources provided for this item in the original record. Removed per default-to-skepticism rule.
No sources provided for this item in the original record. Removed per default-to-skepticism rule.

removed.key: refit_item: aluminum mast refinishing ($2,000-$5,000) — no sources
removed.reason: No sources provided for this item in the original record. Removed per default-to-skepticism rule.

removed.key: refit_item: Webasto diesel heater service ($300-$1,500) — no sources
removed.reason: No sources provided for this refit item, and the underlying Webasto-is-fitted claim was itself removed as unverifiable. Removed.

fabrication_flags: cold.watertight_bulkheads: Quote 'Oyster features watertight bulkheads as part of design for practical cruising capability' attributed to oysteryachts.com/heritage-yachts/oyster-54/ does not appear on that page when fetched. Likely hallucinated.

ergonomics.side_decks: Quote 'Deck Access: Generally favorable; the design accommodates typical cruising operations effectively' attributed to Yachting World review does not appear in that article. Generic template phrasing consistent with hallucination.

tropical.ventilation (YW ventilation article): Quote about Oyster Yachts fitting dorade vents and front saloon windows attributed to yachtingworld.com/practical-cruising/shade-ventilation-keep-yacht-cool-dry-tropics-121676 — fetched page contains no mention of Oyster Yachts whatsoever. Fabricated attribution.

tropical.watermaker: Quote 'No watermaker specifications provided in comprehensive test' attributed to Yachting World review does not appear in fetched article content. Generated placeholder masquerading as a real citation.

parts.availability: Quote 'unique access to the history, original build files, photographic library, and original build yards' attributed to oysteryachts.com brokerage page — fetched page contains no such language. Likely sourced from a now-removed or different page, or hallucinated.

cold.heating: Webasto attribution misapplied — the Oyster service page quote is about air-conditioning/refrigeration/LPG, not heating. The Webasto URL is generic marketing with no Oyster 54 connection. Claim is unsubstantiated inference presented as sourced fact.

ergonomics.mob_recovery: Cruising World quote 'provides good steering control and stability in rescue situations' — the Cruising World URL returned the homepage (not the article) when fetched, meaning this skeg-rudder rescue claim is unverifiable and potentially fabricated.

silent_power page (oysteryachts.com/oyster-life/silent-power/): The source documents a specific Oyster 725 owner retrofit, not a standard Oyster 54 factory feature. Cited in self_steering.electrical_autonomy as if it describes Oyster 54 standard equipment — this is a misleading attribution.
verify.verdict: Significantly degraded from the stated 0.72. Eight fabricated or misattributed quotes identified across five fact entries. Three source URLs returned 403 (unverifiable), two URLs returned homepage/wrong content instead of cited article, and one URL (YW ventilation article) contained no Oyster content at all. Core structural and performance facts (keel spec, mast configuration, fuel capacity, production numbers, 30-knot test, stowage complaints, forward sail locker absence, cockpit increase, freeboard) are well-supported by accessible sources and survive. The major categories of concern are: (1) heating system claim is inferred and misquoted; (2) watertight bulkheads quote is fabricated; (3) side-decks quote is fabricated; (4) dorade-vent attribution is to a page that does not mention Oyster; (5) watermaker quote is a generated placeholder; (6) parts.availability brokerage quote is unconfirmed; (7) standing rigging replacement cost range ($5k-$10k) is materially too low for a 54-foot vessel per the cited source itself. Verified confidence set at 0.52 reflecting roughly half the fact-set's supporting quotes being unconfirmable or fabricated, with the factual core being solid but the detail layer being unreliable.

cold_score.rationale: Re-normalised over sourced inputs only (5 of 7 rubric items have definitive sourced outcomes; hard cockpit shelter and cold tankage are undocumented and excluded from denominator). Keel-stepped mast scores full credit (confirmed, confidence 0.88). Insulation scores partial (cored hull above waterline confirmed at 0.55 confidence; no closed-cell foam specifics verified). Heating scores zero: the Webasto claim was removed as fabricated/unverifiable — no sourced heating system exists for this boat. Inside/sheltered helm scores zero: no dedicated inside helm confirmed; nav station below deck is navigation-only. Watertight bulkheads scores zero: supporting quote was identified as fabricated and removed from verified facts. Raw: 18/80 known-input weight; re-normalised to 23/100. Confidence pulled to 0.52 reflecting half the supporting quotes in this research set being unconfirmable or removed.
cold_score.basis: heating=0/25 (no sourced system); insulation=8/15 (cored hull only, 0.55 conf); inside_helm=0/20 (absent, confirmed); keel_stepped=10/10 (confirmed 0.88); watertight_bulkhead=0/10 (quote fabricated, removed); hard_shelter=excluded (undocumented); cold_tankage=excluded (undocumented). 18/80 known weight re-normalised to 100.

tropic_score.rationale: Re-normalised over 5 of 6 rubric items (light-air SA/D not published in any confirmed source and excluded from denominator). Opening hatches/ventilation: partial credit — opening coachroof windows confirmed (0.60 confidence) but dorade vent attribution was removed as fabricated; 12/20. Shade: cockpit 25% larger than predecessor confirmed (0.72) and extended transom noted, but no bimini, hardtop, or permanent shade structure documented; 6/15. Refrigeration: 'huge fridge space' noted at very low confidence (0.45), no capacity or power specs; 6/15. Watermaker: absent, no factory option, aftermarket only; 0/15. Airflow: spacious three-cabin layout, 15ft 7in beam, opening windows, centre-cockpit design all confirmed (0.72); 10/15. SA/D excluded (no published figure, D/L 211 implies moderate-heavy displacement). Raw: 34/75 known-input weight; re-normalised to 45/100. Confidence reflects average source confidence of 0.57 across tropic inputs weighted by removal of one fabricated ventilation source.
tropic_score.basis: ventilation=12/20 (windows confirmed, dorads removed); shade=6/15 (cockpit size up, no shade structure); refrigeration=6/15 (present, very low conf); watermaker=0/15 (absent); airflow=10/15 (confirmed); SA/D=excluded. 34/75 known weight re-normalised to 100.

stability_score.rationale: Neither AVS nor CE category is confirmed in any verified source. AVS is not publicly specified (confidence 0, no sources). CE Category A is inferred from conservative bluewater design philosophy at confidence 0.35 — this does not meet the threshold for a confirmed designation and the only supporting quote ('a sensible blue water design, the 54 favors the conservative side of the design compromise') is a design-philosophy statement, not a CE category certification. Applying rubric rule: 'Neither -> value 0, basis not published, confidence 0.' The 0.35 inference is noted as colour only.

storm_verdict.text: The only sourced heavy-weather observation is a controlled test sail in 30 knots of breeze conducted by Yachting World, in which the Oyster 54 'power-reached happily under two reefs and 50 per cent headsail' — a positive but limited data point reflecting a single upwind/reaching test, not an offshore passage in storm conditions. The same review notes that the forward double cabin was 'a different story' upwind in a blow, implying motion comfort issues in the bow area during punishing conditions. No published offshore storm encounter reports, circumnavigation storm accounts, or ARC/bluewater rally incident records were found for the Oyster 54 in any verified source. The conservative skeg-hung rudder and keel-stepped mast are design features consistent with offshore storm-worthiness, but their real-world performance in storm conditions cannot be asserted from this evidence base alone.

by_item.note: All 21 Oyster 54s built 2008-2013, now 13-18 years old. Wire rigging life is 10-12 years (all past interval); rod 15-20 years (most approaching interval). Corrected upward from original research estimate: at 54ft, improvesailing.com data implies $15k-$30k range. Replacement likely required on any example.
8-10 year service interval; all boats well past. Halyards, sheets, control lines for a 54ft sloop. Standard rubric range applied.
8-12 year sail life; all boats now 13-18 years old — past interval. Full wardrobe replacement assumed unless recently renewed. Masthead sloop with large genoa and code sail typical for Oyster 54.

25-30 year engine life; 13-18 year old boats not yet at repower threshold. Risk item if high hours — budget for survey-identified major service or injector/heat exchanger overhaul. Full repower ($25k-$70k rubric) not yet indicated; low end reflects contingency only.
10-15 year electronics life; all boats at or past interval. Raymarine ecosystem noted in original research; full suite refresh (chartplotter, AIS, radar, autopilot head) for offshore use.

6-10 year battery life; well past interval on all examples. AGM or lithium bank replacement plus basic solar array for tropical and offshore use. Silent Power page cited Oyster 725 retrofit only — Oyster 54 specific specs not confirmed; rubric range applied.
No anchor/windlass specifications documented in verified sources. Standard rubric range for a 54ft bluewater yacht. Windlass service or replacement likely after 13-18 years.

25-year seacock replacement interval; most boats not yet at interval (13-18 years). Inspection and greasing budgeted; partial replacement if survey identifies stiff or failed valves. Applied lower portion of rubric $3k-$9k range.
Liferaft service every 3 years mandatory; replacement typically needed after 10-12 years. EPIRB, flares, tether/harness refresh for offshore certification. Rubric range applied.
10-15 year canvas life; at or past interval on all boats. No factory bimini or hard shelter confirmed, so any existing canvas is owner-added and may vary in age. Rubric range applied.

No factory watermaker documented in any verified source; absent from standard equipment. Required for tropical passage-making self-sufficiency. 10-20 gal/hr DC reverse-osmosis system. Rubric range applied.

by_item.system: Forward sail locker retrofit (design deficiency correction)
by_item.note: Cited design limitation: no forward sail locker forces crew to retrieve spinnaker/staysail from lazarette on passage — described as 'back-breaking chore' in Yachting World verified review. Custom carpentry solution. Confidence 0.55; labelled optional improvement but practically significant for passage-making.

refit_projection.assumptions: Age logic: all 21 Oyster 54s built 2008-2013; age in 2026 is 13-18 years. Standing rigging cost corrected to $15k-$30k for 54ft vessel per improvesailing.com source (original research $5k-$10k was materially too low per verified facts). Engine repower excluded from primary range (not yet at 25-30yr threshold) but contingency budgeted at $0-$25k. Watermaker included as absent from verified facts. Forward sail locker retrofit included as cited design limitation. All figures are our estimates and represent ranges, not quotes. Actual costs depend on region, yard, and specification of replacement equipment. Survey strongly recommended before purchase to identify which items are already current.

provisioning_specific.item: Spinnaker and staysail retrieval system or dedicated deck bag
provisioning_specific.why: Verified design limitation: no forward sail locker confirmed in Yachting World review. Sails must come from lazarette on passage; a purpose-made deck bag or foredeck retrieval sleeve reduces the 'back-breaking' retrieval problem without full carpentry refit.

provisioning_specific.item: Four oversized dock fenders with dedicated cockpit storage solution
provisioning_specific.why: Verified complaint: fender stowage 'an issue' with four fenders 'frequently tied to the pushpit' per Yachting World review. Provisioning should include a locker bag or stern rail caddy rated for full-size fenders to clear the pushpit.

provisioning_specific.item: Electrical panel access tool and labelled circuit diagram posted at nav station
provisioning_specific.why: Verified issue: main power switches and relays 'positioned under the navigator's seat and only easily accessible to those who enjoy limbo dancing' per Yachting World. A mirror, headlamp, and laminated wiring diagram reduce emergency response time.

provisioning_specific.item: Separate watermaker (aftermarket, 10-20 gal/hr DC unit)
provisioning_specific.why: No factory watermaker confirmed in any verified source. Required for tropical and offshore passage-making self-sufficiency. Absent from standard equipment per verified facts.

provisioning_specific.item: Windvane self-steering (servo-pendulum or auxiliary rudder type)
provisioning_specific.why: Not a factory option per verified facts. Essential for electrical autonomy on long passages — reduces autopilot power draw. Servo-pendulum (e.g., Windpilot, Monitor) or direct-drive (Hydrovane) both compatible per self_steering.windvane_option fact.

provisioning_specific.item: Diesel cabin heating system (Webasto or Eberspacher)
provisioning_specific.why: No factory heating system confirmed in any verified source (original Webasto claim removed as fabricated). A diesel forced-air heater is a standard cold-climate refit for a boat of this size and era; budget $3k-$6k installed.

provisioning_specific.item: Staysail on removable inner forestay (cutter conversion)
provisioning_specific.why: Rigged as sloop Marconi masthead — no cutter staysail confirmed as standard. Yachting World review notes staysail used on passage (confirming the rig can accept one) but stowage was the limiting problem. An inner forestay with stowable hanks or furler provides storm-sail flexibility without the locker problem.

provisioning_specific.item: Forward cabin bunk lee cloths rated for offshore use
provisioning_specific.why: Verified complaint: forward cabin 'challenging upwind in a blow' per Yachting World. Lee cloths on the forward V-berth allow crew to sleep safely during upwind passages in heavy conditions.

provisioning_specific.item: Kit bag storage solution (hanging lockers or compression bags) for cabin clutter
provisioning_specific.why: Verified complaint: 'kit bags and sailing gear clutter cabins' per Yachting World review despite spacious layout. Compression bags and a systematic locker assignment plan for a full crew are essential before extended passages.
